blurb: French restaurant classic — silky vanilla custard with caramelised sugar crust.

prov.popular_in: French restaurants and fine dining globally
prov.famous_for: The classic French vanilla custard with crackling caramelised sugar top
prov.consists_of: Rich egg-cream custard flavoured with vanilla, baked, topped with sugar, caramelised until crisp
prov.name_origin: 'Burnt cream' — originated in Catalonia as crema catalana, adopted into French cuisine
prov.why_loved: The contrast of silky cool custard with warm crackling caramelised sugar is magical
prov.method_detail: Oven — cream infused with vanilla, yolks and sugar whisked, combined, baked in water bath 35-45 min, chilled, sugared, torched

brief.lead: Crème brûlée is the ultimate French dinner party dessert — deceptively simple but requiring technique. A water bath ensures gentle, even cooking for that signature silky texture. The magic happens at the end: a layer of sugar torched until it crackles, creating that satisfying contrast between cool custard and warm caramel.
expect: Proper water bath baking prevents curdling and ensures smooth custard
Sugar torched until amber and crackling

ing: vanilla bean, split and seeds scraped (or 2 tsp extract)
tl: Heat cream with vanilla until just simmering, then steep
Whisk yolks with sugar, combine with cream
Bake at 150°C for 35-45 min until set but wobbly
Refrigerate at least 4 hours or overnight
Sugar top, torch until amber, serve immediately
steps: Infuse the cream
Pour cream into a saucepan. Scrape vanilla seeds into the cream, add the pod. Bring to just below simmer over medium heat, then remove from heat and let steep 15 min. Discard pod.
Infusing extracts maximum vanilla flavour.
Prepare the egg mixture
Whisk egg yolks, sugar, and salt in a heatproof bowl until thick and pale, about 2 min. Do not over-whisk or incorporate air.
Thick yolks create proper custard structure.
Temper and combine

Remove vanilla pod from cream. Reheat cream until just hot. Slowly whisk about 100ml hot cream into the yolks (tempering), then return mixture to saucepan. Cook over lowest heat, stirring constantly, until slightly thickened and coating the back of a spoon (80-82°C on thermometer).
Tempering prevents scrambling eggs; gentle cooking thickens without curdling.
Strain and fill ramekins

Pour custard through fine-mesh sieve into a jug (removes any cooked bits). Divide evenly among 4 ramekins. Place ramekins in a baking dish. Pour boiling water into the baking dish to reach halfway up the sides of ramekins.
Straining ensures silky texture; water bath provides gentle, even heat.
Bake until set
Bake at 150°C for 35-45 min until edges are set but centre still wobbles like jelly (it will firm as it chills). Remove ramekins from water bath immediately to stop cooking.
Low, gentle heat prevents curdling; residual heat completes cooking.
Chill thoroughly
Cool at room temperature 30 min, then refrigerate uncovered 1 hour until cold. Cover and refrigerate at least 4 hours or overnight — custard must be completely cold for best caramelisation.
Proper chilling develops texture and ensures sugar crackles properly.
Caramelise the tops

Just before serving, blot any condensation from custard surface. Sprinkle 1 tbsp sugar evenly over each custard, tilting to coat. Shake off excess sugar. Using a kitchen torch, caramelise sugar until deep amber and bubbling, moving flame constantly. Cool 2 min until sugar hardens and crackles.
Even sugar layer ensures proper crackle; torch creates signature caramel crust.
Serve immediately
Serve within 10-15 min while sugar is still crisp. The contrast of warm crackling caramel with cool silky custard is the magic.
Timely service preserves the signature texture contrast.

blurb: Italy's coffee classic — espresso-dipped ladyfingers with mascarpone cream, dusted with cocoa.

prov.popular_in: Italian trattorias and dessert menus globally
prov.famous_for: The iconic Italian no-bake dessert with coffee-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone
prov.consists_of: Savoiardi (ladyfingers) dipped in espresso, layered with mascarpone-cream mixture, cocoa-dusted
prov.name_origin: Tiramisu = 'pick me up' — from coffee kick and sugar energy; invented in 1960s Veneto
prov.why_loved: The coffee-soaked biscuits with silky mascarpone cream and bitter chocolate dust is perfection
prov.method_detail: No-bake — egg yolks whipped with sugar, mascarpone folded, ladyfingers dipped in espresso-rum, layered, chilled 4+ hours, cocoa-dusted

brief.lead: Tiramisu is Italy's most famous dessert — a perfect balance of strong coffee, rich mascarpone cream, and bitter cocoa. The key is the texture contrast: crisp ladyfingers soften just enough in the coffee mixture, while staying supported by the silky cream. Proper chilling time is non-negotiable — this dessert needs 4+ hours to meld flavours and achieve that iconic fork-tender texture.
expect: Properly whipped and folded for light, rich texture
Dipped, not drowned — ladyfingers should be tender, not mushy
Espresso provides the classic Italian kick

tl: Brew espresso, add rum if using, cool completely
Whisk yolks with sugar until thick, fold in mascarpone
Dip ladyfingers, layer with cream, repeat, finish with cream
Refrigerate at least 4 hours or overnight
steps: Prepare coffee mixture
Brew strong espresso and cool to room temperature. If using rum or brandy, stir in now. Add 2 tbsp sugar, stirring until dissolved. Pour into a shallow dish wide enough to dip ladyfingers.
Cool coffee prevents melting mascarpone; shallow dish ensures even dipping.
Make the yolk base

Whisk egg yolks and sugar in a heatproof bowl over a pan of simmering water (double boiler) until thick, pale, and doubled in volume — about 5 min. Remove from heat and whisk until cool (or use electric whisk 3-4 min). This stabilises the cream.
Cooked yolks are safer and create stable structure.
Fold in mascarpone

Add vanilla to yolk mixture. In a separate bowl, whisk mascarpone briefly to loosen (do not overwhisk or it will split). Fold mascarpone into yolk mixture in 3 additions until combined. If using double cream for stability, whip to soft peaks now and fold in.
Gentle folding keeps cream light and prevents splitting.
Dip ladyfingers
Working quickly, dip each ladyfinger into coffee mixture for exactly 1-2 seconds per side — they should be moistened but not soggy. Do not soak.
Quick dipping creates tender layers without mushiness.
First layer
Arrange dipped ladyfingers in a single layer in your dish (break to fit if needed). Spread with half the mascarpone cream, smoothing top.
Even layers ensure consistent texture throughout.
Second layer
Repeat dipping and layering ladyfingers. Top with remaining mascarpone cream, spreading smoothly. Cover tightly with cling film.
Double layering creates proper depth of flavour.
Chill thoroughly
Refrigerate at least 4 hours, preferably overnight. This is essential — ladyfingers soften completely and flavours meld into that classic tiramisu texture.
Proper chilling is non-negotiable for authentic texture.
Finish and serve
Just before serving, sift cocoa powder generously over the top. Cut into squares and serve cold. The cocoa provides the perfect bitter-sweet balance to the rich cream.
Cocoa dusting completes the classic flavour profile.

blurb: The French bakery classic - flaky, buttery crescents of laminated dough.

prov.famous_for: The ultimate French pastry - 27 layers of buttery flaky dough
prov.consists_of: Laminated yeast dough with butter, shaped into crescents, baked until golden
prov.name_origin: Austrian kipferl adapted in France; crescent shape celebrates victory
prov.why_loved: Buttery flavour, shatteringly crisp layers, tender interior, French breakfast perfection
prov.method_detail: Oven - dough laminated with butter, proved, baked at 200°C until golden

brief.lead: Croissants are the pinnacle of French patisserie — a testament to patience, precision, and butter. The magic lies in lamination: folding butter into dough to create paper-thin alternating layers that puff into golden flaky crescents. This is a 2-3 day process: make détrempe (dough) day 1, laminate day 2, prove and bake day 3. Temperature control is critical — butter must stay cold but pliable throughout. The reward? 27 layers of buttery perfection that shatters at first bite.
expect: Proper lamination creates signature flaky texture
Egg wash creates deep mahogany colour
Proper proving creates open, airy crumb
The smell is half the magic

tl: Mix dough, knead 8 min, refrigerate overnight
3 single folds + 1 double fold (or 4 single folds), refrigerate between
Roll, cut, shape crescents, prove 2-3 hours at 25°C
Egg wash, bake at 200°C for 15-20 min until deep golden
steps: Make the détrempe (Day 1)

Mix flour, sugar, yeast, and salt in a bowl. Add warm milk and melted butter. Mix until shaggy, then knead on a floured surface 8 min until smooth and elastic. Shape into a rectangle, wrap tightly, refrigerate overnight (12-16 hours).
Overnight resting develops flavour and relaxes gluten.
Prepare butter (Day 2)
Pound cold butter between baking paper into a 15cm square, about 5mm thick. Keep it cold but pliable — it should bend without cracking. Refrigerate if too soft, let warm 5 min if too stiff.
Proper butter consistency is critical for clean lamination.
First fold (single)

Roll détrempe on a lightly floured surface into a 45x15cm rectangle. Place butter square on one half, leaving 2cm border. Fold other half over butter, sealing edges. Roll away from you to 45x15cm. Fold into thirds like a letter (single fold). Wrap, refrigerate 30 min.
First fold creates 3 butter layers; resting prevents gluten tearing.
Second fold (single)

Roll dough again to 45x15cm (short edge toward you). Fold into thirds (single fold). Wrap, refrigerate 30 min. Repeat this process 2 more times for a total of 4 single folds. (Alternatively: 3 single folds + 1 double fold for different layer structure).
Each fold triples layers: 4 single folds = 81 theoretical layers (reduces to ~27 actual).
Rest overnight
After final fold, wrap dough tightly and refrigerate overnight (8-12 hours). This rest is crucial — dough must be fully relaxed before rolling to prevent butter streaking.
Overnight rest ensures even butter distribution and clean lamination.
Shape croissants (Day 3)

Roll dough on floured surface to 60x20cm rectangle, about 4mm thick. Trim edges. Cut diagonally into 8 isosceles triangles (base 12cm, height 20cm). Cut a 2cm slit in base of each triangle. Roll from base to point, curving ends into crescents. Place on baking trays, leaving 8cm space.
Proper shaping and spacing ensures even rising and full crescent form.
Prove
Cover loosely with oiled cling film. Prove at 25°C for 2-3 hours until doubled in size and wobbly when tray is shaken. Dough should hold a fingerprint without springing back.
Slow, cool proving develops flavour and prevents butter melting.
Bake
Preheat oven to 200°C. Brush croissants with egg wash (avoid pooling). Bake at 200°C for 15-20 min until deep golden brown and cooked through (internal temperature 94°C). Cool on wire rack 10 min.
Egg wash creates colour; high heat ensures proper puff and crispiness.
Serve
Best served warm the same day. Reheat day-old croissants at 160°C for 5 min to refresh. Ideally served with butter and jam, or alongside café au lait.
Fresh croissants are at their peak — store in airtight bag, refresh as needed.
